IEEE COMMUNICA TIONS SURVEYS & TUTORIALS, VOL. XX, NO. X, XXX 2017 1
Routing Attacks and Mitigation Methods
for RPL-Based Internet of Things
Ahmed Raoof, Student Member , IEEE, Ashraf Matrawy, Senior Member , IEEE,
and Chung-Horng Lung, Senior Member , IEEE
Abstract—The recent bloom of Internet of Things (IoT) and
its prevalence in many security-sensitive environments made the
security of these networks a crucial requirement. Routing in
many of IoT networks has been performed using the Routing
Protocol for Low Power and Lossy Networks (RPL), due to
its energy-efﬁcient mechanisms, secure modes availability, and
its adaptivity to work in various environments; hence, RPL
security has been the focus of many researchers. This paper
presents a comprehensive study of RPL, its known attacks, and
the mitigation methods proposed to counter these attacks. We
conducted a detailed review of the RPL standard, including a
recently proposed modiﬁcation. Also, we investigated all recently
published attacks on RPL and their mitigation methods through
the literature. Based on this investigation, and to the best of our
knowledge, we introduced a ﬁrst-of-its-kind classiﬁcation scheme
for the mitigation methods that is based on the techniques used
for the mitigation. Furthermore, we thoroughly discussed RPLbased Intrusion Detection Systems (IDSs) and their classiﬁcations, highlighting the most recently proposed IDSs.
Index Terms—RPL, Internet of Things, Routing Attacks, IoT,
IoT Security, IDS, Attacks Classiﬁcation, Mitigation Methods
Classiﬁcation.
I. I NTRODUCTION
Internet of Things (IoT) is a network of things that are
uniquely identiﬁed and connected to the Internet. These things
can range from Radio Frequency Identiﬁcation (RFID) tags
and small sensors and actuators in Wireless Sensor Network
(WSN), smart grids and Machine-to-Machine (M2M) networks, all the way up to smartphones and connected vehicles.
Hence, IoT networks are heterogeneous and use various standards [
1], [ 2]; one of which is the IPv6 over Low-powered
Wireless Personal Area Network (6LoWPAN).
6LoWPAN networks have the following main characteristics
[3], [ 4]: resource-constrained nodes (energy, memory, and
processing power), lossy links, and low data rates ( ∼ 250kbps).
In addition, Most of the trafﬁc in these networks is either pointto-multipoint (root to leaf nodes) or multipoint-to-point (leaf
nodes to root node). Besides, these networks use 6LoWPAN
protocol stack [
5], developed by Internet Engineering Task
Force (IETF). This protocol stack is based on IPv6 protocol
[
6], with the addition of an adaptation layer (6LoWPAN) [ 7],
[8] that handles header compression, and fragmentation and
reassembly of IPv6 packets (among other duties). Fig. 1 shows
a comparison between 6LoWPAN’s and TCP/IP’s protocol

stacks. Additional details about other protocols and standards
used in this protocol stack can be found in [
3], [ 9], [ 10].
As WSN routing methods do not work effectively in IoT
[4], [ 11], and the traditional Internet routing protocols are
not suitable for the resource-constrained IoT devices, several
solutions have been proposed for routing in 6LoWPAN networks (and IoT in general) [
12]–[15]. Among these standards,
Routing Protocol for Low Power and Lossy Networks (RPL)
was introduced by Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)
[
3]. RPL is designed from scratch to meet the routing needs
of IoT networks and minimize resource consumption along the
routing path. The protocol is also ﬂexible to adapt to different
environments it works within; this is done by using a suitable
Objective Function (OF) – see §
III. It also has several energyefﬁcient repair mechanisms.
IoT has gained tremendous attention and security is one
of the major concerns in IoT. Moreover, RPL is designed
explicitly for IoT networks (including smart grids and M2M
networks) to meet the requirements of routing and efﬁcient
resource consumption. Hence, there is a critical need to investigate the security aspect of RPL for a better understanding of
the attacks and mitigation techniques for RPL.
In this survey, we will focus on the routing attacks on
RPL and their mitigation methods, whether these mitigation
methods are part of the current protocol implementation/design
or proposed in the literature. We summarize our contributions
as follows:-
• We have investigated and presented a thorough review of
RPL’s speciﬁcations, including a recent "security-minded"
proposal to update and modify the standard [
16]. To our

best knowledge, to the time of this writing, there is no
other survey covers this recent update.
• For the attacks themselves, we have investigated all new
and recent literature (up to the beginning of 2018) to
include the latest updates. Further, in order to facilitate
better understanding and differentiation between these
attacks, a classiﬁcation based on the origin of the attacks
was also devised and presented in the paper.
• Moreover, we introduced a classiﬁcation for RPL’s attacks mitigation methods. To our best knowledge, this is
the ﬁrst time such a classiﬁcation is conducted, which
provides an easier way to understand and track current
and upcoming mitigation proposals.
• IDSs were discussed with great details on their working
principles, and also were updated with their latest proposals/extensions.
We are targeting researchers in the security of RPL-based
IoT networks, whether beginners to the ﬁeld or experienced.
In our opinion, the contributions mentioned above will provide
the targeted readers with an integrated survey to keep up
with most recent RPL updates and current research directions,
which are not available (in one survey) in the current literature
[
2], [ 4], [ 12]–[15], [ 17]–[21]. We believe that readers of our
work would learn the following lessons:-
1) For the beginners, they will grasp the main concepts of
RPL, its control messages, and security features, in addition to understanding RPL’s strengths and limitations.
The review of RPL is presented in §
III.
2) Our work presents a one-stop place where targeted
readers can ﬁnd the latest information on current routing
attacks on RPL-based networks. This includes inherited
traditional attacks from WSN, known attacks that are
based on RPL’s own vulnerabilities, and new altered
versions of both types. A classiﬁcation of these attacks,
along with the attacks’ details and mitigation methods,
are discussed in §
IV -A,§VI, and § VII.
3) Our novel classiﬁcation for the mitigation methods of
RPL attacks would help any researcher better understand
the different ways to mitigate attacks, and provide an
insight on the research direction for each attack’s mitigation. This classiﬁcation is detailed in §
IV -B.
4) To aid researchers who are working on IDSs systems for
RPL, our work introduces a well-organized section on
the most inﬂuencing IDS that have been proposed for
RPL-based networks. This can be found in §
V.
A highlight of the most inﬂuencing surveys is represented
in the following paragraphs:-
Wallgren et al. in [ 4] provided a thorough survey on many
of RPL attacks, including how RPL responds to these attacks
via simulation results. In addition, they supplemented their
survey with a heartbeat mechanism to mitigate selectiveforward attacks – see §
VI-A. However, the survey only covers
attacks inherited from WSNs. Also, authors did not provide
details of the used mitigation methods.
Granjal et al. in [
12] made a well-organized survey on
6LoWPAN networks and its set of protocols, including RPL.
They described many details regarding RPL and the security
measures included in its speciﬁcations. In addition, the authors
presented a list of research challenges for each protocol used
in the protocol stack. However, no attacks were discussed for
any of the protocols, nor recent updates to the standards.
Pongle and Chavan in [
19] illustrated a short survey on
attacks against RPL and the 6LoWPAN adaptation layer. They
also included a brief discussion on IDS classiﬁcation used to
mitigate RPL attacks. However, only a short description of
mitigation methods has been provided, and the authors did
not include the latest proposals nor standard updates.
Airehrour et al. in [
14] provided a comprehensive survey
on the security of RPL. The survey included a study on trustbased methods used to mitigate routing attacks. However, most
of these mitigation methods are used mainly for WSNs rather
than RPL-based IoT.
Alaba et al. in [
15] conducted a thorough survey on current
security issues in IoT and their taxonomy. However, the paper
did not discuss the speciﬁcs of the attacks on RPL.
Kim et al. in [
21] reviewed 97 RPL-related papers, covering
many research directions. This well-organized survey brought
a classiﬁcation for the reviewed literature based on subtopics (e.g., multicasting, security, etc.), and provided some
detailed statistics on the reviewed papers (e.g., the number of
papers specialized in each sub-topic, RPL implementations,
etc.). However, in regard to RPL’s security, the survey only
dedicated one page (out of 24) to talk about current research
on RPL’s security. Unlike our work, the authors (in this onepage) only listed nine types of attacks, then moved to brieﬂy
discuss the literature they reviewed in regard to mitigating such
attacks. Since the survey is not security-focused, there was no
RPL review, no classiﬁcation for neither the attacks nor their
mitigation, and no IDSs were discussed. Also, the survey did
not look into the recent drafts and proposals by the IETF.
Liu et al. in [
22] made an in-depth performance analysis on
RPL in large-scale networks. The authors reviewed the main
concepts of RPL working principles and its control messages,
then studied some of existing and proposed OFs. Simulations
in OMNeT++ [
23] were conducted (using between 100 to
600 nodes for the experiments) to evaluate RPL’s performance
under the studied OFs. The paper included a short discussion
on RPL’s security. However, and in comparison to our work,
authors only listed six types of attacks, with little information
on some mitigation methods used against them. In addition,
IDSs were mentioned as their selected-option to mitigate
RPL’s attacks, but the paper did not provide any details nor
evaluation on such proposed IDSs.
The rest of this survey is organized as follows: Section
II shows a brief comparison in routing between WSNs and
RPL-based networks. Section III presents a comprehensive
review on RPL, its working principle, DODAG creation and
maintenance, self-healing mechanisms of RPL and its builtin security features. Classiﬁcation of RPL’s routing attacks
and their mitigation methods are held in section
IV. Section
V thoroughly discusses the proposed IDSs for RPL-based
IoT networks. A comprehensive study on routing attacks on
RPL according to their classiﬁcation is conducted in sections
VI & VII. Our vision on the current issues, challenges, and
future work (in regard to mitigating RPL’s routing attacks) is

II. WSN S AND IOT: S IMILARITIES AND DIFFERENCES
Due to the fact that most of IoT concepts are coming from
WSNs, there is a lot of confusion between the two. In general,
WSNs and IoT networks share various similarities in their
characteristics [
11], [ 13], [ 24]:-
• Nodes are deployed depending on the network application: deployment can be deterministic or random, mobile
or static. This situation creates a signiﬁcant challenge
when routing the data for all trafﬁc types – see §
III-C.
• The resources of nodes are constrained: usually many
of the nodes depend on batteries or energy-harvesting
technologies to operate, and most of them have limited
storage/memory capacities and low processing powers.
This requires lightweight protocols to be implemented.
• Networks may work in harsh conditions: lossy links and
nodes that go on and off due to energy conservation
schemes or simply are exhausted. In addition, a large
number of nodes may be scattered and sometimes mobile.
Such conditions require routing protocols to be scalable
in a fast, energy and resource efﬁcient ways.
• Due to their constraints, nodes are highly susceptible to
attacks, internal or external.
However, WSNs and IoT networks also have their differences: ﬁrstly, WSNs are designed originally [
1] to coordinate
data collection and activate actuation, without any intelligence
at the nodes (all processing and decision making is done outside the sensor network). On the contrary, IoT is designed to
introduce smartness to the things, e.g., having more powerful
nodes (within the IoT network) that can handle some of the decision making tasks. IoT also presents a direct communication
between each node and the Internet, so intensive processing
can be carried out in the cloud and response is sent back
directly to that node.
The second difference lays in the fact that IoT networks
use the IP protocol stack. This feature also means that routing
in IoT networks is based on IP addresses, which are a global
addressing scheme. However, this is not the case for WSNs
which use different techniques for routing, as explained in the
following paragraph.
There has been a signiﬁcant amount of research on routing
protocols for WSNs. Recent surveys conducted by Kumari
and Prachi [
24], and Singh and Singh [ 11] presented a comprehensive overview of these protocols. Fig. 2 shows a general
taxonomy of WSN routing protocols, as classiﬁed by [ 11].
The classiﬁcation was performed based on the structure of
the network and the routing concept: In Flat-based routing
protocols, all nodes have the same functionality and sensing
is performed by a collaboration between a large number of
nodes. The routing here is conducted by queries from different
regions of the network based on data types. Hierarchicalbased routing, on the other hand, divides the network into
clusters; each has at least one Cluster Head (CH) which is
responsible for processing and aggregating data collected from
other cluster member nodes and transmitting the aggregated

The details of how WSN routing protocols work are out of the
scope of this survey as they have been thoroughly investigated

III. RPL O VERVIEW
RPL was developed by IETF ROLL workgroup to provide
routing service in low power lossy networks (LLN), where
devices are highly constraint in resources. It is a distance
vector routing protocol that organizes network devices into
Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) [
25]. A DAG represents a
network where all nodes are connected in a way such that there
are no round-trip paths and trafﬁc is routed to reach one or
more root nodes. In the DAG resides one or more DestinationOriented DAG (DODAG), in which the topology has one root
node only (usually is the gateway or border router "6BR")
and where usually all the data are sinked [
3], [ 12]. To enable
several applications to work simultaneously, but independently,
inside the network, several RPL instances can co-exist inside
a DAG; each can have one or more DODAG. All DODAGs
inside an RPL instance share the same RPLInstanceID and use
the same Objective Function (more on that below) [
3]. Fig. 3
shows an illustration of a DAG with two RPL instances and
three DODAGs.
Before going further into RPL operations, it is essential
to clarify a few critical terms: an Objective Function (OF)
deﬁnes essential conﬁgurations such as routing metrics used,
optimization objectives, how to calculate the rank, and how to
select parents in the DODAG [
26]. Since there are multiple
and different applications for IoT deployment, link attributes
that are important for each application are different from the
others; hence, the OF will be different. The details of the
requirements for each of the typical IoT applications can be
found in [
27]–[30], but they are out of scope of this survey.
There are currently two fully-deﬁned and three draft OFs, set
by IETF:-
• Objective Function Zero (OF0): OF0 simply adds a
predetermined value to the previous rank value [
31].
• Minimum Rank with Hysteresis Objective Function
(MRHOF): It selects routes that minimize a metric, while

using hysteresis to reduce churn in response to small

• EXT Objective Function (EXTOF) - Draft: This OF
selects paths that minimize the number of packet transmissions for packet delivery [
33]. However, EXTOF is an
expired Internet draft, even though it is still widely used
in many IoT networks.
• Load Balancing Objective Function (LBOF) - Draft:
LBOF adds Child Node Count (CNC) as a metric, and
uses it to select paths in a way that maintain a balanced
number of children per preferred parent in the DODAG
[
34]. This will balance the trafﬁc between the nodes,
resulting in lower power consumption (hence longer
network lifetime), a lower possibility of bottlenecks, and
better delivery rate. An evaluation for this OF was carried
in [
35] with a comparison to OF0 and MRHOF, and it
shows that LBOF provides longer network lifetime (by
16-40%) and better delivery rate (by 10-15%). However,
with larger networks the LBOF seems to consume more
energy due to parents churn. The current draft of LBOF
suggests using a combination of routing metrics to reduce
this churn.
• Trafﬁc Aware Objective Function (TAOF) - Draft: TAOF
uses a combination of EXT and Packet Transmission Rate
(PTR) as routing metrics, and uses it to select paths with
less trafﬁc toward the root [
36].
In addition to these OFs, each RPL implementation can have
its own OF.
The second term is Node’s Rank , which is an unsigned
integer value deﬁnes node’s relative position to DODAG’s root
node. It is calculated using routing metrics deﬁned in the OF –
could be as simple as actual distance or hop count, or complex
by including other properties such as link cost, link/node status
and nodes constraints – and it strictly increases going down
the DODAG (root node always has the lowest rank) [
3]. Rank
is used by nodes to decide what set of parents to choose and
the preferred parent, as will be discussed in § III-B.
Other terms are the DODAGID, which is the IPv6 address
of DODAG’s root node, used as a unique identiﬁer for the
DODAG inside an RPL instance, and DODAG V ersion, which
deﬁnes a speciﬁc iteration "version" of the DODAG with
a DODAGID. As will be discussed later, DODAG V ersion
Number is a counter used to keep up with DODAG’s version,
and it is incremented only by DODAG’s root node.
The following sections cover important RPL features: control message types used in RPL, the formation of the DODAG
and downward routing tables, self-healing mechanisms of
RPL, and RPL’s security features.
A. RPL Control Messages
There are ﬁve types of control messages used in RPL; each
has two versions: base and secure (except the Consistency
Check message, which has only a secure version). In the
following, base versions of these messages are listed:-
1) DODAG Information Object (DIO) Message: This
message is advertised by each node when it wants to join a
DODAG, create a DODAG (root node only), or to maintain
the DODAG. It contains information that is used to identify
RPL’s instance (RPLInstanceID), DODAG’s ID, DODAG’s
version number, RPL’s mode of operation, the rank of sending
node, DODAG conﬁguration (including the OF adopted), and
other information and options [
3]. Usually, DIO messages
are multicasted by nodes once they receive DIO messages
from other nodes. The only exception is the root node, as
it starts the multicasting process and it is the only node to
set up necessary information, excluding the rank, which is
computed by each node before transmitting its own DIO
message [
4], [ 5]. In addition, a DIO message should be sent
when a DODAG Information Solicitation (DIS) message is
received [
3].

2) DODAG Information Solicitation (DIS) Message: DIS
message is used by nodes when they want to join a DODAG,
and they did not receive any DIO messages for a period of
time. It basically probes its neighbors looking for available
DODAGs. The response to DIS message depends on how it
was sent: if unicasted then each receiving node will unicast
a DIO message to the sender with DODAG conﬁguration
and will not reset its trickle timer; if multicasted then each
receiving node will reset its trickle timer and broadcast
DIO message to the neighboring nodes [
3]. The use of DIS
messages, however, does introduce a vulnerability which
could be used to start an attack – see §
VII-D. IETF is
intending to solve this problem by introducing some ﬂags in
the DIS message [
16].
3) Destination Advertisement Object (DAO) Message
and Its Acknowledgment (DAO-ACK): While RPL uses
DIO and DIS messages to create and maintain the upward
routes (toward the root node)in the DODAG, it uses DAO
duo to ﬁnd the downward routes (from the root or parent
node toward children or leaf node) [
3], [ 12]. DAO contains
path information for reachable nodes by its sender; this
information is used to create routing tables at receiving nodes
for point-to-point (P2P) and point-to-multipoint (P2MP -
downward) communications. Sending and responding to DAO
messages depends on RPL’s mode of operation – see §
III-C
–, in storing mode DAO messages are unicasted to node’s
parent(s), while in the non-storing mode it will be unicasted
toward DODAG’s root node [
3]. Depending on the ﬂags ﬁeld
of the DAO message, the receiver may respond by sending
an acknowledgment message (DAO-ACK).
4) Consistency Check (CC) Messages: CC messages are
used by RPL to synchronize security counters/timestamps
between each pair of nodes and provide a challenge-response
mechanism as a basis for control messages’ reply attacks’
protection [
3], [ 12] – see § III-E. These messages are always
sent as secured messages, hence only available in RPL’s secure
modes. For more details and an example on when to use these
messages, see §
III-E.
B. DODAG F ormation and Maintenance
As mentioned in § III-A1, DODAG formation is done by
exchanging DIO messages, and it always starts from the root
node. The root node should set most of the DIO base ﬁelds:
RPL instance ID, DODAG ID, DODAG version, RPL mode
of operation – see §
III-C – and sets its rank as ROOT_RANK
[3], which is equal to the minimum rank increase per hop
"MinHopRankIncrease".
Upon receiving DIO messages [ 3], [ 5], [ 14], each node
should: (i) calculate its own rank (using the speciﬁed OF and
based on the information received from the DIO message),
(ii) decide on which DODAG to join (if there are multiple),
(iii) select at least one preferred parent from a set of possible
parents, and (iv) multicast its own DIO message. In the new
DIO message, the node should change only the rank – from
the DIO base ﬁelds – to reﬂect its own, while maintaining all
the rest as originally set by the root node [
3].
According to the current RPL speciﬁcations [ 3], each node
– except the root node – should maintain a set of possible
parents, which it selects from the list of its neighbors based
on their rank and the OF: their ranks should always be lower
than its own. From this set, the lowest ranked parent is chosen
as the preferred parent. In case of having more than one parent
with the same lowest rank, based on the implementation and
OF, the node may select all or some of them as preferred
parents. The use of rank to select the parents prevents the
creation of routing loops.
In order to maintain the topology, DIO messages should be
exchanged on a regular basis, and nodes may decide to discard
it if the new DIO message does not result in any changes to the
current conﬁgurations of the DODAG at receiving node (e.g.,
DODAG version change) or a change in the preferred parent
of it [
3]. To reduce the effect on nodes’ limited resources, RPL
employs Trickle algorithm [ 37] to control when to send DIO
messages [3], [14]: each node will maintain a DIO counter and
a trickle timer, with a pre-deﬁned threshold for the former
and trickle time for the latter. DIO message will be sent
when the trickle timer reaches its time or a DIO message that
causes changes in RPL conﬁguration. Now, whenever a DIO
message is received and discarded, DIO message counter will
be increased. If the counter reaches a threshold value, both the
counter and trickle timer will be reset and trickle time will be
doubled. In addition, DIO counter and trickle time will return
back to their original settings when a change is made due
to a received DIO message. This procedure allows for fewer
broadcasts of DIO messages when the network is stabilized,
and for quick topology update when there is a change [
14].
C. Downward Routes and RPL Modes of Operation (MOP)
RPL does support the three types of communications in
6LoWPAN networks (see § I): Multipoint-to-Point (MP2P);
which resembles the upward trafﬁc, Point-to-Multipoint
(P2MP); which resembles the downward trafﬁc, and Pointto-Point (P2P); resembling the trafﬁc between two nodes who
none are root nodes of the DODAG.
The ﬁrst type, MP2P , is guaranteed by the DODAG topology
created using method explained in the previous section, while
P2MP is done by the root node (as it keeps the full downward
routing table). For P2P , it has to be done using one of three
ways [
3], [ 25]:
1) Non-Storing Mode ; where trafﬁc goes all the way up
to root node, which will be responsible for sending it
toward its destination using source routing [
38] (see
Fig.4a), and
2) Storing Mode ; in which each node will keep downward
routing table for its sub-DODAG and use it to forward
P2P trafﬁc. In other words, trafﬁc will go upward until
it reaches a common ancestor (of the sender and destination) which will route it downward to the destination
node – see Fig.
4b.
3) In addition, RPL provides the ability for one-hop P2P
trafﬁc [ 3].
As mentioned in § III-A3, DAO messages are used to create
the downward routing table. Depending on RPL’s mode of
operation [
3]:-

• In storing mode, each node will send DAO message to its
DAO parent (which must be one of its possible parents
set). Receiving nodes should use information contained
within DAO message to create and maintain downward
routing tables. This process should be done by all nonleaf, routing-capable nodes only.
• In non-storing mode , nodes will send DAO messages
toward their DODAG root node, which is responsible
for creating and maintaining source routing tables using
received DAO information.
According to RPL’s speciﬁcations, both modes cannot be
active at the same time and are selected by the DODAG
root node only. Also, there are no mechanisms to assure that
routing-capable nodes are storing/maintaining entries for all
of their sub-DODAG nodes [
3].
D. Self-Healing Mechanisms of RPL
Current RPL speciﬁcation includes a number of self-healing
mechanisms: one global repair mechanism and several local
ones. The Global Repair mechanism occurs typically when
the root node ﬁnds signiﬁcant inconsistencies in the DODAG
[
5], [ 25]. At that time the root node broadcast a DIO message
with an incremented DODAG version. This step will start the
regeneration of the DODAG and force all nodes to discard
the current DODAG formation, reset their trickle timers and
start joining the new DODAG. The only node allowed to start
a global repair is the root node [
3]; however, the number
of inconsistencies or their degree of severity that trigger the
mechanism are left for the implementation.
The main Local Repair procedure for the downward paths
consists of resetting DIO trickle timers and exchanging updated DIO messages. It also includes poisoning the subDODAG (by sending DIO message with Rank ﬁeld set to
INFINITE_RANK) [
3], which forces child nodes to detach
the sending node from being a preferred parent and selecting
another one. It is called local due to the fact it only affects
the sub-DODAG of the nodes.
Another local repair procedure occurs when a node ﬁnds
out its preferred parent is no longer available (either through

link layer acknowledgments, not receiving DIO messages for
an extended period of time, or after receiving a poisoning
DIO message). The node then will have to select another
preferred parent from its possible parents set, and if none were
available or the set was empty it will start the main local repair
procedure [
3], [ 39].
Beside these procedures, two recovery mechanisms are also
available, which can be summarized into the following [ 3],
[40]:-
• DAG Inconsistency Loop Recovery: DODAG inconsistency is detected whenever the direction of a packet
contradicts rank relationship between the sender and
receiving node. I.e., a packet with Down "O" ﬂag is
set but the sender is of higher rank (packet is directed
downward but received as upward), or a packet with
Down "O" ﬂag clear but the sender is of lower rank
(packet is directed upward but received as downward)
[
3], [17]. This may occur during a global repair procedure
(rebuild of DODAG with the increase of DODAG version
number) [
3] when a node still on the previous DODAG
receives a packet from a sender whom already joined
the new DODAG with a changed rank value. Another
possibility for having a loop is when a node wants to
detach from the DODAG, and some of DIO messages
get lost or not delivered, then this particular node joins
the same sub-DODAG with a different rank [
3].
RPL has a repair mechanism for this situation, called
DAG Inconsistency Loop Recovery , in which receiving
node forwards the packet with Rank-Error "R" ﬂag set.
Whenever a receiver detects DODAG inconsistency, it
will check the "R" ﬂag of the inconsistent packet: if
the ﬂag is clear, the receiver will set it and forward the
packet. If the ﬂag is set, the receiver will drop the packet,
reset DIO trickle timer and start a local repair procedure
(sending updated DIO messages to its neighbors) [
3].
• DAO Inconsistency Loop Recovery: When a node
receives a packet from a parent node that is targeted
toward one of its child nodes (or their children), it checks
the routing table to ﬁnd the path. If one of these routes
(that had been learned from a previous DAO message)
is no longer available, it will create inconsistency in the
network as other nodes are still not aware of the change
[
17].
RPL has an optional repair mechanism for this situation called DAO Inconsistency Loop Recovery [
3]: the

packet will be returned to the parent of the node with
Forwarding-Error "F" ﬂag set and Down "O" ﬂag left
unchanged (set). When the parent receives a packet with
F ﬂag set, it will remove routes to the destination through
that neighbor, clear the "F" ﬂag from the packet and send
it to the destination again through a different neighbor.
The process will recurse if that neighbor also has inconsistency or will end by delivering that packet - See Fig.
5.
It worth noting that this process is not applicable for nonstoring RPL mode, as only the root node has a routing
table and it uses source routing. If a route is not available,
an "Error in Source Routing Header" ICMP message is
sent back to the root.
E. RPL Security Features
According to RPL own speciﬁcations [
3], the protocol has
several security features, which are listed below. However, the
speciﬁcation states that implementing these security features
(partially or fully) is "optional":-
1) RPL is designed to use link-layer security mechanisms
when they are available to secure message transmission.
2) Beside link-layer security, RPL has its own security
mechanisms, provided mainly through three security
modes:-
• Unsecured Mode: The default mode for RPL when
it depends on link-layer security. In this mode,
no security measures are applied to RPL control
messages.
• Preinstalled Mode: Preinstalled keys (symmetrical
keys) are manually preconﬁgured on the nodes,
which uses these keys to handle and generate the secure versions of RPL control messages when joining
the DODAG and for its maintenance. This mode is
recommended when secure routing is desired with
a lot of constrained devices.
• Authenticated Mode: Nodes want to join the
DODAG as leaf nodes use the preinstalled keys to
join (similar to preinstalled mode), but nodes with
routing capabilities must use the preinstalled keys
to obtain another key from authentication authority.
The process of which the authority is authenticating
the nodes or how to distribute the keys is left for
the implementation.
3) RPL also has an optional " Replay Protection " mechanism, called Consistency Checks. These checks use a
non-repetitive value (CCM Nonce) (which is sent within
the CC secure messages – see §
III-A4) and the stored
status information (originating node and CCM nonce
counter last received from that node) to check if the
received CCM nonce value has been used before from
the originating node; hence, the mechanism detects a
replay attack.
In the second and third modes, secure versions of RPL
control messages are exchanged. To support these messages’
conﬁdentiality, integrity, and authenticity, RPL uses AES/CCM
(the Advanced Encryption Standard in "Counter with Cipherblock chaining Message authentication code" mode) with 128bits key to generate 32-bit and 64-bit Message Authentication
Code (MAC). These MACs are used to assure the integrity of
the messages. Also, RPL uses RSA with SHA-256 for digital
signatures of the messages, to provide the conﬁdentiality and
authenticity, with optional 2048 and 3072-bit signatures.
It is worth noting that, up to this moment, the current implementations of RPL use it in the unsecured mode. Even the
most commonly used operating systems (Contiki OS [
41] and
TinyOS [42]) do not have these security features implemented
in their RPL implementations. Perazzo et al. in [ 43] provided
a partial implementation of RPL’s security features by adding
the preinstalled mode and the replay protection to ContikiRPL
(Contiki OS implementation of RPL). The authors evaluated
the performance of their implementation via simulations in
Contiki, and showed that implementing RPL’s preinstalled
mode by itself (no replay protection) will not add signiﬁcant
increments to network formation time, RPL’s control overhead,
or power consumption. On the other hand, implementing replay protection mechanism would induce signiﬁcant delays to
network formation time and control overhead, which also leads
to higher power consumption, especially in large networks
(with more than 25 nodes).
IV. C LASSIFICATION OF RPL A TTACKS AND MITIGATION

sections.name: Methods
sections.content:
A. Classiﬁcation of RPL’s Attacks
As mentioned in §
I, IoT has some similarities to WSNs
but it extends the communication with the Internet all the
way down to the things. This means that IoT networks inherit
attacks from both of WSNs and the traditional Internet-based
networks [
12]. In this survey, the focus is on routing attacks
that RPL is susceptible to, which can be classiﬁed (based on
attack’s origin) to attacks inherited from WSNs and attacks
based on RPL own vulnerabilities (RPL-speciﬁc attacks).
These two classes of attacks, along with speciﬁc common
attacks that belong to each class, are illustrated in Fig.
6. A
comprehensive discussion of these attacks, along with their
mitigation methods is presented in §
VI & § VII.
B. Classiﬁcation Scheme for RPL Attacks Mitigation Methods
Through this survey, mitigation methods can be divided
into mitigation mechanisms and IDSs. Mitigation mechanisms
usually are either added procedures to RPL standard or
modiﬁcations to the current RPL procedures. In general, the
mitigation mechanisms are used to deal with speciﬁc types or
limited numbers of the attacks (normally one or two types)
but have lower consumption to nodes’ resources. On the other
hand, IDSs are designed more toward detecting and mitigating
several types of attacks at once, and can be extended to cover
attacks that they were not originally designed to detect. In
addition, IDSs require some degree of collaboration between
the nodes.
In order to better understand the mitigation methods for
RPL’s attacks (both mechanisms and IDSs), we created a
classiﬁcation scheme for these mitigation methods based on
the various techniques adopted. This classiﬁcation scheme is
presented as follows:-

• Acknowledgment-based Methods: The concept of these
methods depends on sending a message and receiving
an acknowledgment. This message should be signed in a
way to prevent alteration. An example is the Heartbeat
protocol [
4], where the root node sends an ICMPv6 echo
message to each node and waits for an acknowledgment.
If nothing is received from that node, it will be considered
as a malicious node. In general, these methods are not
effective by themselves and are often used in combination
with other methods.
• Trust-based Methods: Nodes have to monitor their
neighbors, rate them using some algorithm and create
trust relations based on these ratings. This type is usually
effective combating WSN inherited attacks and some of
the RPL speciﬁc attacks, such as the decreased rank attack
– see §
VII-A.
• Location-based Methods: These methods relate nodes
or packets to their locations within the network, whether
geographically; e.g., using GPS systems, or logically;
e.g., via Round Trip Time (RTT) calculations or using
information from the Link/PHY layers. A good example
would be Packet Leashes [
44]. Currently, these are the
only reliable methods to detect wormhole and Sybil
attacks – see §
VI-C & VI-D.
• Statistical/Mathematical-based Methods: Here detection/mitigation is based on statistical or mathematical
models. For example, Surendar and Umamakeswari proposed an IDS [
45] that uses Evidence Theory to detect
sinkhole attacks – see § VI-B.
• Speciﬁcation-based Methods: These methods use RPL’s
own speciﬁcations (such as rank and DODAG version)
to detect the attacks. An example would be the work of
Glissa et al. in [
46] that ﬁnds a rank threshold value based
on monitored DIO messages, and uses that to detect rank
attacks – see §
VII-A.
The speciﬁc methods that belong to each class are described
later in this survey. This includes the following section, which
presents more detailed information about IDSs and the current
proposals for IoT networks.
V. C URRENT TRENDS OF IDS S FOR RPL-B ASED IOT
IDSs are generally classiﬁed based on either their detection
method or their placement strategy [
47]. Detection methods
divide IDS into:
• Signature-based IDSs: These IDSs use a database of
signature patterns of the attacks to detect them [ 19]. However, even though it consumes less resources than other
types of IDS, it is not efﬁcient in detecting unregistered
attacks.
• Anomaly-based IDSs: These IDSs monitor the trafﬁc
of the network to create a normal behavior proﬁle, then
compare network activities to this proﬁle and consider
any anomaly as a possible attack [
47]. Normally these
IDSs are efﬁcient in detecting known attacks and possibly
new ones that cause anomalous behaviors. However,
they usually have false positive/false negative detections,
which limits its potentials. Also, they are considered more
resources consumers than the previous type.
• Speciﬁcation-based IDSs: Similar to anomaly-based detection methods, these IDSs also create a normal behavior
proﬁle of the network. However, they differ in that this
type of IDSs creates the network proﬁle based on network
(or protocol’s) speciﬁcations, normally deﬁned manually
by the operator [
19], [47]. This results in much lower false
positives and false negative rates than that of anomalybased methods, and almost no training period. On the
other hand, using a manual deﬁnition of the speciﬁcations
makes it harder to adapt to environment’s changes. Le et
al. in [
48] introduced a semi-auto proﬁling technique for a
speciﬁcation-based IDS that uses trace ﬁles to help create
the rules for their IDS.
• Hybrid IDSs: Combining two of the methods mentioned
above in one IDS creates a hybrid system that usually
takes their advantages and minimizes the drawbacks [
47].
On the other hand, using IDS placement for classiﬁcation,
IDSs can be divided into:-
• Centralized IDS Placement: Here the IDS resides either
on the root node (e.g., border router) or a dedicated host
(e.g., cluster head node) [
47], and uses the trafﬁc going
through to detect attacks. In many cases, it is required
that the central node of the IDS send periodic requests
for updates from the monitored network. Advantages of
centralized IDS are that most of the heavy work occurs
inside a powerful node, which provides the ability to
perform extensive security checks. Also, it is usually
capable of protecting the network from the Internet-side
attacks and botnets, due to performing as a ﬁrewall. On
the other hand, it would be challenging to monitor the
network during the attack itself.
• Distributed IDS Placement: In this type of IDSs [
47],
[49], each node will have a full IDS implementation,
making it responsible for detecting attacks around it.

Usually, there is a collaboration among the nodes to increase the efﬁciency of attacks’ detection and mitigation.
However, this approach results in high consumption of
resources on all IoT devices. Hence, another approach is
used: distributing monitoring nodes ( watchdogs) within
the network who are responsible for the monitoring task,
then they communicate with IDS units inside other nodes
to mitigate the attacks. The main advantage of these IDSs
is better monitoring and detection of insider attacks. Since
major parts of the IDS, namely the decision making, are
implemented within each node, this generally results in
higher resources consumption at the nodes. It is usually
required to optimize the IDS periodically to minimize this
effect.
• Hybrid IDS Placement: To get the best of both previous
placement strategies, a hybrid solution is desirable [
4],
[19], [ 47]: central node(s) that have more resources
and are responsible for computationally intensive IDS
tasks (such as analyzing data gathered from monitoring
nodes, decision making, etc.) and normal nodes that are
responsible for performing lightweight IDS duties (such
as monitoring neighbor nodes, sending data about trafﬁc
passing through them, and responding to mitigation control messages from central nodes). This approach has the
advantage of better and faster detection of the attacks than
the centralized approach, and having a lower resources
consumption compared to the distributed approach. However, optimization should be performed on the IDS, and
the placement of central nodes should be chosen wisely,
depending on the application of the network and its
environment.
In the IoT paradigm, and due to the highly constrained
nature of the devices, a hybrid detection approach with hybrid
placement IDS is the most preferred option for implementation. As it is clear that this combination reduces resource
consumption and increases the detection rate, and that is
what most of the current research is working on [
4], [ 14],
[19]. There are many proposals for IDSs for IoT and WSNs;
however, only a few of them stands out because of their
features that are suitable for RPL-based IoT.
In the following, a summary of these most inﬂuential
IDS for IoT, designed more speciﬁcally for RPL-based IoT
networks, is provided.
A. SVELTE
This is a speciﬁcation-based, hybrid detection, hybrid placement IDS, proposed by Raza et al. [
50]. The system consists
of three modules:-
• 6LoWPAN Mapper (6Mapper): Residing on the root
nodes, this module is responsible for constructing a full
DODAG with each node’s neighbors and parent information.
• Intrusion Detection Module: This module also resides
on the root node. This module is responsible for detecting
the attacks using both signature-based and anomaly-based
methods, mainly by using the rank and DODAG information collected by the 6Mapper module. It is designed
with the ability to be extended to cover newer attacks,
as claimed by the authors. Also, it includes several
algorithms to reduce false positive and false negative
rates.
• Distributed Firewall and Response Module: This module is installed on every node. Its responsibility is to
prevent attacks from outside the network, even when
the data is encrypted. Also, it is responsible for sending
information requested by the 6Mapper module.
One of the requirements of SVELTE is to use all secure modes at the Application layer (Constrained Application
Protocol, CoAP , with Datagram Transport Layer Security
protocol, DTLS), the Network layer (6LoWPAN with IPSec
enabled), and the Link layer (encryption enabled). This is to
assure indistinguishability for IDS control messages.
The original version of this IDS showed promising results
(80 to 90% true positive rate, as demonstrated by the authors)
against blackhole, selective-forward, sinkhole, and DODAG
inconsistency attacks – see §
VI & VII. An extended version
[51] added geographical hints (as location information) and
introduced a new metric (Expected Transmission Count -
ETX), to lower the false positive and false negative rate, and
to provide detection ability for Sybil and wormhole attacks.
As pointed out by many researchers (Le et al. in [
48],
Alzubaidi et al. in [ 52], and SVELTE’s authors [ 50], among
many others), the original SVELTE IDS tends to have more
false positives and false negatives in medium to large IoT
networks. This is due to the way SVELTE detects the " invalid
inconsistencies": it depends on the number of reported ranks
of the nodes, then it uses a simple threshold (the difference
between the ranks of the nodes) to detect such inconsistencies
(normally the threshold is set to 20%). This shortcoming has
been investigated intensively by many researchers [
45], [ 47],
[49], [ 53].
B. Intrusion Detection of Sinkhole Attacks on 6LoWPAN for
the Internet of Things (INTI)
This IDS is a statistical/mathematical-based, anomaly detection, hybrid placement IDS proposed by Cervantes et al. [
49]
to overcome original SVELTE’s drawbacks: not supporting
node mobility [
49], high false positive rates (when there are
many attacks, as per the authors) [ 54], and high resource consumption (speciﬁcally energy and memory [ 53]). The system
divides the network into clusters. Each cluster consists of one
Leader, at least one Associated Node (a node that receives
messages from another leader in order to forward it toward
the root node), and Member Nodes (normal nodes who joined
the cluster and do not have a connection to another leader).
Then, using the rank and nodes statistics, leaders calculate
reputation and conﬁdence values for each node in their cluster.
These values are used to detect and isolate adversaries in the
network by applying evidence theory.
The system was evaluated with the sinkhole attack, showing
results that are similar to the ones obtained from SVELTE,
but with lower false positive rate [
47], [ 49]. However, unlike
SVELTE, INTI does support nodes mobility. Energy consumption was not investigated and it should have been due to the

high number of calculations performed within the system.
Authors also mentioned the ability to extend their system to
detect other types of attacks. However, they did not disclose
any details in regard.
C. Intrusion Detection and Response System (InDReS)
Proposed by Surendar and Umamakeswari [
45] as an
enhancement to INTI – see § V -B, this IDS is also
a statistical/mathematical-based, anomaly-detection, hybrid
placement system. The system divides the network into clusters, within each cluster a node is elected as a Leader based
on probability. All nodes in the cluster will send their rank
to the cluster leader, who will use it to detect and isolate
the attacker(s) based on Dempster-Shaffer evidence theory.
Whenever an attacker is detected, the root node will be notiﬁed
by the cluster leader. The root node then will reconstruct the
DODAG, excluding attacker nodes from it.
The system was designed originally to detect and mitigate
sinkhole attacks only. However, the authors claimed that their
system is extendable to cover other types of attacks, such as
rank and version number attacks. Unfortunately, no details on
such extensibility were provided. The effect of node mobility
is also yet to be investigated.
D. Game Theory IDS
Based on an original concept, Sedjelmaci et al. proposed [
54] a statistical/mathematical-based, hybrid-detection,
distributed-placement IDS. Their concept is to use the
signature-detection method to detect common attacks, while
anomaly-detection is used only when the trafﬁc is suspected
to be malicious. The use of either method is decided by setting
a game between IDS entities and the attackers: anomalydetection is trained by turning on periodically in the beginning,
and it is triggered if trafﬁc pattern hits a threshold set up by
applying gaming theory to the learned patterns. The authors
also combined their system with reputation system to minimize
the false positive rate.
The IDS was evaluated against a sinkhole attack. The evaluation showed similar results to that obtained from SVELTE
but with lower energy consumption. Detections of other attacks
(wormhole, blackhole, Sybil, etc.) were also described in the
original design. However, they were never evaluated, nor for
node mobility.
E. RPL Speciﬁcation-based IDS
This is a unique IDS, proposed by Le et al. [
48]. This IDS
is considered as a speciﬁcation-based, speciﬁcation-detection,
hybrid placement IDS. A proﬁle of RPL speciﬁcation for routing is generated by the IDS, using trace ﬁles, and this proﬁle
is used to detect and mitigate various attacks. The IDS was
originally designed for sinkhole, rank, local repair, neighbor,
and DIS attacks, but due to its design it can be extended
to cover other attacks as well, as claimed by its authors.
Using this approach, a network will be divided into clusters,
each with a cluster head that has the IDS agents, which are
responsible for the proﬁling, detecting, and mitigation of the
attacks. Simulation results showed high detection rate with a
small percentage of false positive and false negative detections
(between 0-7%), while energy consumption was up by 6.3%
only. However, the authors have not considered the effect of
mobility in their investigation, which would be a challenge to
the proﬁling stage of their IDS.
F . Distributed IDS for V ersion Number Attacks Detection
Introduced in [
53] by Mayzaud et al. , this speciﬁcationbased, signature-detection, hybrid-placement IDS is designed
mainly for the detection and mitigation of version number
attacks. Several " monitoring nodes " are distributed strategically inside the network to monitor DIO messages, check
DODAG version number within them, and prepare a " Possible
Attackers" list based on the fact that a version number change
should not come from a higher rank node before lower ones.
These lists are then sent periodically to the root node where
they are aggregated and a curated list of the attackers is
created. This ﬁnal list is distributed to all nodes for exclusion
(isolating the attackers).
The authors conducted an evaluation for this IDS. The
experiment results showed excellent detection rates. However,
it was also shown that to minimize the false positive detections; each normal node should be monitored by at least three
monitoring nodes.
G. Real-time IDS for Wormhole Attacks Detection
In general, to detect and mitigate wormhole attacks is to use
location-based methods, and this IDS is not an exception. Pongle and Chavan proposed location-based, signature-detection,
hybrid placement IDS [
55] to detect two types of wormhole
attacks: Packet Encapsulation and Packet Relay – see § VI-C.
The system works as follows: the root node will have
all nodes’ locations and their transmission ranges pre-stored.
Each node then will periodically send information about their
neighbors and the Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)
to the root node. The root node then uses all the information to
calculate the distances between the nodes and determines if a
new neighbor has been formed but out of the transmission
range, which means a wormhole attack. It also can ask
individual nodes to monitor their surrounding nodes and report
RSSI information to pinpoint adversaries.
Simulation evaluation of the IDS showed promising detection rate (94% for attack only detection and 87% for both
attack detection and pinpointing the attacker) with very low
resource usage. However, there was a higher number of control
messages than normal RPL, due to the nature of the IDS.
In addition, The IDS is not effective with mobile nodes,
according to the authors themselves. The authors mentioned
the possibility to extend the IDS for detecting other attacks,
namely Sybil, blackhole and selective-forward, version number
and local repair attacks. However, details of these extensions
were not discussed nor evaluated.
H. SPRT-based IDS for Selective-F orwarding Attack Detection in IPv6-based Mobile WSNs
Based on the Sequential Probability Ratio Test (SPRT)
statistical method [
56] combined with an adaptive threshold,

Gara et al. [57] proposed this statistical/mathematical-based,
anomaly-based, hybrid-placement IDS to detect malicious
nodes performing selective-forward attacks – see §
VI-A in
RPL-based networks with mobile nodes.
The IDS consists of two modules: a central decision making
module at the node node, and distributed monitoring-andresponding modules at routing nodes. The distributed modules
hold a neighbors table that records the number of packets sent
and received from each neighbor. Then, these statistics are
sent periodically toward the central module at the root node in
form of Hello messages. These Hello message are sent through
random paths (other than the preferred parent) to mitigate the
possibility of being ﬁltered by a malicious parent.
Using these information, the central module analyze the
packets sent and received by each node and its parent, to
determine if one of them is lying (hence detecting a falsiﬁcation attack against the IDS). After the analysis, SPRT is
used to determine if each node is legitimate or malicious.
Once all the decisions is made, the elimination of the detected
malicious nodes is conducted by initiating a global repair
using a modiﬁed DIO message. This modiﬁed version of DIO
contains the malicious nodes’ identiﬁers. Each receiving node
then should discard the malicious nodes from it parent list,
which results in full isolation to the adversaries.
The authors performed an evaluation for their IDS using
simulations (through Cooja, the simulator of Contiki OS),
and it showed that the IDS is very capable of eliminating
the selective-forwarding attacks, with detection rate reaching
%100 with some parameters optimization. However, this excellent performance comes at the expense of having much high
overhead trafﬁc (due to the Hello message), which was noted
by the authors. It is our understanding that such overhead
would increase drastically with larger networks and would
slow them to a crawl. In addition, such high overhead trafﬁc
results in much higher energy consumption; hence, shortening
the life of the IoT network signiﬁcantly. Our last observation
points that the IDS does not use any encryption, including the
new Hello messages. This may introduce a new threat as the
adversaries can take advantage of the lack of the encryption
and send fabricated Hello messages on behave of their victims;
thus, hindering the detection process.
Summary and Insights
From the discussion above, it is clear that there is a lot of
work in regard to introducing IDSs for IoT networks, more
speciﬁcally IDSs for RPL. Our observations shows the following: The dominant theme for IDS placement is the hybridplacement system, as it minimizes resources consumption. In
regard to IDS detection methods, no theme is dominant (in this
survey, we have discussed equal number of IDSs per detectionmethod). However, both speciﬁcation-based and hybrid-based
detection methods showed higher rates of detections with
medium-to-lower resources consumption than signature-based
IDSs, which suits the constrained nature of IoT devices.
From mitigation method point of view, the most commonly
used methods are the statistical/mathematical-based methods,
with speciﬁcation-based methods coming second. It was also

VI. WSN-I NHERITED ATTACKS
Since a large part of IoT is originated from WSNs, many
of WSN routing attacks have migrated to IoT networks, with
small changes in their methods to cope with IoT paradigm.
These attacks are discussed in the following sections.
A. Blackhole & Selective-F orward Attacks
In a blackhole attack [
19], malicious node(s) will drop all
packets it receives, instead of forwarding them, resembling a
"blackhole" in the network and causing a Denial of Service
(DoS) - see Fig.
7. A more sophisticated attack would forward packets from selected protocols and drop the rest; e.g.,
forwarding RPL control messages and ICMPv6 packets, and
dropping everything else. This is known as Selective-F orward
attack [
4] or Greyhole Attack. It has been shown in [ 58] that
blackhole attacks by themselves are not very effective, unless
a large number of adversaries were deployed in a strategical
manner, as RPL self-healing features will eventually remove
blackhole adversaries from the topology. However, combining
blackhole attacks with other types of attacks; e.g., sinkhole
attacks, it can degrade the quality of service in the network
and even results in DoS to some parts of the network.
The main difference between blackhole and selectiveforward attacks relies in their purpose: Blackhole attack is
intended to create a DoS inside the network, while SelectiveForward’s target is to make a big disruption in routing paths
[
4], [ 19]. Because of their nature, selective-forward attacks
cannot be detected nor mitigated by the self-healing mechanisms of RPL, as malicious nodes usually pass RPL’s control
messages and participate in DODAG creation and maintenance
as any legitimate node [
4], [ 19].
Mitigation techniques: There have been a lot of work to
mitigate blackhole and selective-forward attacks, with mainly
IDSs used for blackholes and encryption for selective-forward

follows:-
1) Using source routing, or creating a disjoint path between
leaf nodes and the root minimizes the effect of selectiveforward attacks [
4]. However, it is not easily applicable
to large networks.
2) Also, using encrypted packets mitigate selective-forward
attacks, as it eliminates attacker’s ability to identify the
used protocol. Hence adversary either allows all trafﬁc
or turns itself into a blackhole [
19], but this will increase
the End to End (E2E) delay.
3) Acknowledgment-based methods: Two methods have
been proposed:-
• Parent Fail-Over mechanism: Proposed by Weekly
and Pister [ 59] as part of their solution to mitigate
a combined sinkhole and blackhole attack. In this
mechanism, root node marks a node as a blackhole
if it did not hear from it for 10 seconds, and sends
a list of these "Unheard Nodes" in a modiﬁed DIO
message to all the nodes to blacklist it. However,
and according to the authors, parent fail-over is not
efﬁcient when blackhole attack is combined with
Sybil attack.
• Heartbeat mechanism: Proposed by Wallgren et al.
in [
4] to detect/mitigate selective-forward attacks.
With this mechanism, an ICMPv6 echo message is
sent from the root node to each node and it expects
a reply. It is advised by the authors to use IPSec
with Encapsulated Security Payload (ESP) to get
the full potential of the protocol and prevent attacker
node(s) from identifying ICMPv6 messages.
4) Trust-based methods: several solutions were proposed,
including:-
• Airehrour et al. in [
60] proposed a trust-based mechanism to detect and isolate blackholes. Each node
will calculate " Trust V alues" for all its neighbors
(by monitoring their transmissions), then uses these
values to choose its preferred parent. However, it is
necessary to investigate their mechanism’s efﬁciency
when mobile nodes are involved, as authors used
static topology for their evaluation.
• The work of Khan and Herrman in [
61] proposes
a distributed mechanism that also ﬁnds trust values.
However, these values also are sent to the root node
where they are aggregated into " reputation values",
which in turn are used to identify adversaries. In
addition, trust values are affected by the results of
several checks, including " F orwarding check" for
selective-forwarding and blackhole attack detection.
• Djedjig et al. in [
62], [ 63] proposed a trustbased version of RPL. Their proposal requires
a Trusted Platform Module (TPM) co-processor
to be implemented within each node to handle
the extra cryptography. Nodes within the network
will compute Extended RPL Node Trustworthiness
(ERNT) for their neighbors based on several parameters, such as energy, rank and neighbor’s in1

B. Sinkhole Attack
For this type of attacks, malicious nodes try to be sinks
for as much trafﬁc as possible by advertising a fabricated
route with better metrics. This will attract nearby neighbors
to send their trafﬁc through the adversaries - see Fig.
8.
By itself, sinkhole attacks are not disruptive, but when they
are combined with other types of attacks (e.g., blackhole or
wormhole attacks); where the passing trafﬁc can be altered,
forged or used for spooﬁng; the new mixture makes a very
powerful attack.
Usually, this attack can be performed in several ways: by
advertising a DIO with a better combination of the rank
and the OF – see §
III (this version of sinkhole attack is
synonymous to the decreased rank attack in RPL, see § VII-A).
Other ways can be through manipulating preferences or having
several adversaries directing all passing trafﬁc toward another
adversary (instead of their preferred parent as in the DODAG),
creating a sinkhole. However, to our best knowledge, no
literature was found discussing these methods; hence, it should
be investigated.
Mitigation techniques:
1) Trust-based Methods: The same trust-based methods
used to mitigate blackhole attacks are also valid for
sinkhole attacks’ mitigation – see §
VI-A.
2) Statistical/Mathematical Methods:-
• Weekly and Pister [ 59] proposed two mechanisms
to countermeasure sinkholes/blackholes. Part of that
solution is "Rank V eriﬁcation" mechanism, which
uses one-way hash-chaining to verify topological
ranking.
• IDSs of this type, such as InDReS (see §
V -C), Game
Theory IDS (§ V -D) and INTI (see § V -B) can be
used to detect and mitigate sinkhole attacks.
3) Speciﬁcation-based Methods: SVELTE (§V -A) and RPL
speciﬁcation-based IDS (§ V -E) have been proved effective against sinkhole attacks. Also, the mechanism
proposed by Glissa [
46] (described in § VI-A6) also is
used for sinkhole attacks mitigation.
C. Wormhole Attacks
Usually, two malicious nodes cooperate in this attack, as
they create a tunnel [ 4], [19] between them and transmit trafﬁc
(entirely or selectively) through it instead of the regular path
that would be dictated by the DODAG if that tunnel did not
exist – See Fig.
9.
In general, there are three ways to create a wormhole [ 55]:-
• Packet Encapsulation [55], where malicious nodes use a
legitimate path between then and create a logical tunnel
by encapsulating original legitimate packets to hide hop
count from other nodes on tunnel’s path.
• Packet Relay [
14], [55], in which one (or more) malicious
node(s) relay packets between two far legitimate nodes to
deceive them into being neighbors, usually this is done
by relaying packets without changing the hop count.
• Out-of-Band Link [
4], [ 14], here malicious nodes use an
out-of-band link (wired or wireless) to communicate with

Perazzo et al. in [
65] conducted an evaluation of wormhole
attack effects using actual testbed. The implemented wormhole
was done via an out-of-band link and their evaluation was
based only on packet loss. The results from the evaluation
showed a signiﬁcant increase in the packet dropping, due
to lost RPL control messages which causes longer recovery
delay for the affected nodes. In addition, the authors provided
a critical analysis to some of the countermeasures used to
mitigate wormhole attacks (e.g. addition of specialized hardware such as GPS, making modes monitoring their neighbors,
timeouts measurements, etc.), and they argued that none of
these countermeasures provide a practical solution, due to
the extra costs or the high impact on the devices’ resources.
However, the authors didn’t support their argument with any
solid evidence, nor did an actual evaluation of such mitigation
methods through their testbed network. In our opinion, there
should be more work on such evaluation, for example to
include energy consumption and E2E delays effects.
Mitigation techniques: Out-of-Band wormholes are very
difﬁcult to detect and still present a big challenge [
4], but for
the other two approaches of the wormholes, there are few ways
to detect them [
4], [ 55]:-
1) Location-based Methods:
• Geolocation-Based methods: Tying up geographical
information to nodes and neighborhoods of the
network make it much easier to detect and stop a
wormhole [
4], for example: including GPS hardware
inside the nodes – or using indoor location beacons
– and integrating the geographical location into
path authentication [
55]. However, attackers could
easily deceive this method by sending misleading
location information. Besides, this method still faces
difﬁculties identifying mobile attackers.
• Packet-Leashes methods: First introduced in [
44],
packet leashes simply constrains the trip of packets
to speciﬁc limits within the network (geographical

D. Clone ID & Sybil Attacks
In Clone ID attack, a malicious node(s) takes the identity
of another legitimate node. A more sophisticated attack, the
Sybil attack, can be performed with each malicious node
taking several identities from legitimate nodes [
4], [19]. Zhang
et al. [64] classiﬁed Sybil attacks according to their social
connections and mobility into three types (it is to be noted
that each malicious node can take and use several identities):-
• SA-1, in which malicious nodes are organized in one area
with more bonds between them than the legitimate nodes.
The main goal of this type is to manipulate data; e.g., in a
sensing system it can steal the identity of many legitimate
nodes and submit forged information to manipulate the
system. Also, in SA-1 all the nodes are ﬁxed. See Fig.
10a.
• SA-2, This is more common in sensing networks, where
malicious nodes are distributed among legitimate nodes
and not bond to one area. Also, this type is much
harder to detect as malicious nodes have many "socially
normal" connections with the legitimate nodes. The main
goals of this type of attack in RPL-based IoT networks
are to disturb the routing topology and manipulate any
reputation-based system. As with SA-1, all the nodes here
are ﬁxed. See Fig.
10b
• SA-3, V ery similar to SA-2, but here the nodes have
the ability to be mobile, which reﬂects on their social
connections: they do not have long relationships with their
neighbors. The goals are the same as for SA-2, but the
discovery of these nodes is harder due to mobility. See
Fig.
10c
Besides that, malicious nodes can use stolen identities from
legitimate nodes, or use fabricated ones to communicate.
However, detecting and eliminating the fabricated ones is
somewhat easier by using whitelists. Also, malicious nodes
can use all of their identities at the same time ( Simultaneous
Sybil) or use a subset ( Non-Simultaneous Sybil ) [
70].
Medjek et al. [70] made an analytical analysis to estimate the impact of Sybil attacks in RPL, with mobility in
mind. It has been shown that a Sybil attack will signiﬁcantly increase control overhead (due to repeated DODAG
repair/construction), decrease packet delivery (either control
messages take priority, or packets are delivered to malicious nodes instead of the original legitimate nodes), and
increase energy consumption (due to the increased overhead

Mitigation techniques: The best way to mitigate Clone
ID/Sybil attacks is by keeping track of instances of each
node/identity, better with geographical location info, as two
instances of the node cannot be at two locations at the same
time [
4]. Another way is to use Distributed Hash Table (DHT)
to follow the instances [ 19]. Other methods are summarized
in the following:-
1) Trust-based Methods: Zhang et al. [64] proposed the
following trust-based schemes to detect and mitigate
Sybil attacks in Social IoT
1 [71]:-
• Social Graph-Based Sybil Detection (SGSD): This
scheme depends mainly on creating a "social" graph
of the network to identify the malicious Sybil nodes.
The detection of the adversaries is based on two
assumptions: (i) Sybil adversaries have tight connections with each other and limited connections with
legitimate nodes, and (ii) Sybil nodes cannot have
tight connections with several legitimate nodes in
several social communities. Each node will implement either one or both of these assumptions to build
trust relationships with its neighbors. By looking
back at the characteristics of Sybil attack types,
scheme’s two assumptions represent an excellent
match to SA-1 characteristics. Hence, this scheme
is effective against SA-1 type [
64].
• Behavior Classiﬁcation-Based Sybil Detection
(BCSD): In this scheme, the behavior of the
nodes within the social graph is the basis for
detecting Sybil nodes. The assumption here is
that adversaries will follow limited, repetitive, and
speciﬁc behaviors compared to normal users, even
though they may have several, tight connections
with legitimate nodes in several social communities.
Due to the similarities between the characteristics
of type SA-2 Sybil attack and the assumption used
in this scheme, this detection method is effective
against type SA-2 [
64].
• Mobile Sybil Defenses: Mainly developed to mitigate type SA-3, these schemes swap the need for a
global social graph with other methods to overcome
the decentralized nature of mobile IoT networks:-
– Friend Relationship-Based Sybil Detection
(FRSD): In these schemes, trust relationships are
built by maintaining a "friends" list at each node.
This list is based on speciﬁc social parameters;
e.g, nodes belonging to one community or
performing similar tasks. However, it was
mentioned in [
64] that these schemes require
knowledge of the communities and ways of

relationships between IoT devices in a way similar to how humans make
social relationships [
71].
identifying nodes’ community in advance, which
is not efﬁcient.
– Cryptography-Based Mobile Sybil Detection
(CMSD): These schemes are based on using
crypto-identities or pseudonymous identities and
signatures to make trust relationships and hence
detect Sybil nodes.
– Feature-Based Mobile Sybil Detection (FMSD):
Using network features or characteristics to detect
Sybil nodes is the basis for these schemes. For
example, receiving packets from several nodes
with identical received signal strength (RSS) and
direction would mean that these are stolen identities of a Sybil node, or detecting that several
nodes are transmitting with the same RSS and
are moving together.
2) Speciﬁcation-based Methods: By adding extensions that
collect geographical information or hints [
51], both
SVELTE (see § V -A) and RPL speciﬁcation-based (see
§V -E) IDSs can use this information to build a network
proﬁle that would be able to detect Sybil/cloneID attacks.
3) Statistical/Mathematical-based Methods: As mentioned
by their authors, Game theory IDS (see §
V -D) and
InDReS (see § V -C) also can be used to mitigate the
attacks via extended versions of the IDSs. The ﬁrst one
can have an extended version of its hybrid detection
system that observes the signal strength distribution
around each node, then creates a signature pattern for
the network to detect Clone ID/Sybil attacks [
54]. On
the other hand, InDReS can be extended by adding
behavior monitoring and introducing numerical analysis
to its detection algorithm [
45].
E. HELLO Flood Attacks
When any node wants to join a network, it usually starts by
broadcasting a "HELLO" message. In RPL, DIO messages are
considered as HELLO messages. An attacker can send DIO
messages with strong routing metrics and/or strong signal; then
it might just disappear or reduce its transmission power to
normal [
4], [ 19]. This is known as Hello Flood Attack. If the
malicious node announced itself using stronger transmission
power then disappeared or reduced its transmission power,
packets from legitimate nodes would fail to deliver [
14]. Another effect of such attack is the exhaustion of legitimate nodes
and network congestion due to increased control overhead.
Mitigation techniques: RPL’s self-healing mechanisms
(both local and global) can mitigate this attack. This is done
by using the link layer metrics to calculate the best route:
if no acknowledgment is received, the path is considered
bad and a different parent is selected [
4], [ 17], [ 19]. Also,
using geographical information to identify the location of
the originating node for each packet can help mitigate this
attack: if the originating node is out of transmission range,
all of its packets will be dropped. However, combining this
type of attacks with other types will increase the difﬁculty of
mitigating them [
19].

Summary and Insights
It can be seen that, by their own, most of the WSN-inherited
attacks does not have a big effect, with the exception of
the wormhole and Sybil attacks which can cause noticeable
degradation to the network’s Quality of Service (QoS), in
addition to being much harder to detect and mitigate due
to their nature. However, combining two or more attacks
could lead to signiﬁcant impact on the network and devices’
resources. For example, in many cases sinkhole attack is
combined with other types of attacks (wormhole, selectiveforward, neighbor attacks, etc.) to withdraw larger amounts of
trafﬁc toward the adversary, resulting in bigger impact.
Our observation shows that using one of RPL’s secure
modes can prevent adversaries from joining the network;
hence, mitigate all the aforementioned WSN-inherited attacks.
However, these secure modes will not be of same effectiveness
if the adversaries have access to the encryption keys used, as
they would join the network as any legitimate node.
From mitigation methods’ point of view, location-based
methods are the main ways to be used for wormhole and
Sybil attacks, while trust-based methods provide decent mitigation against sinkhole and, in the same time, introduce
another layer of protection against some of Sybil attacks
variations. In addition, IDSs that use speciﬁcation-based or
statistical/mathematical-based methods proved to be capable
of mitigating several types of the WSN-inherited attacks, while
not taxing the scarce resources of the IoT devices.
VII. RPL-S PECIFIC ATTACKS
These attacks are based on characteristics/features of RPL
protocol, such as the rank and version ﬁelds in RPL’s control
messages, repair mechanisms, etc. Since these attacks modify
control messages, they also known as Alteration and Spoof
Attacks. The following sections summarize the literature on
these attacks.
A. Rank Attacks
As mentioned earlier, each node chooses its preferred parent
based on two values: its Rank and Objective Function (OF),
which are distributed by DIO messages [
3]. The rank should
increase going downward in the DODAG, and the role of the
preferred parent selection is to select the one with the best
rank. An adversary can manipulate these values, as explained
below, and hence severely affect routing topology [
4], [ 14],
[19], [ 72]. It is worth noting that rank attacks, as well as
sinkhole attacks, are combined with other types of attacks
[
4] most of the time, such as selective-forward attack (see
§VI-A) or IP spooﬁng [ 73], to increase the attack impact on
the network.
This manipulation can be done in two ways: changing the
adversary’s rank by a speciﬁc value, based on the ranks of
the adversary’s neighbors. This is sufﬁcient if no security
measures were applied (such as an monitoring IDS). The other
way is by manipulating adversary’s rank through the use of
a different OF (at the adversary) to deceive legitimate nodes
into giving the malicious node better rank. Using this method
makes the adversary harder to detect [
39], [ 72] as it can adapt
to any changes in the topology dynamically. In the following,
we summarize the types of rank attacks [
17]:-
• Decreased Rank Attacks: Malicious node(s) advertises
lower rank (with or without changed OF) to other nodes,
resulting in many of them selecting the adversary as their
preferred parent (see Fig.
11b). This is basically a sinkhole
attack - see § VI-B.
• Increased Rank Attacks: Contrary to the normal behavior
of malicious nodes, adversaries here will be near the root
node, but advertise higher rank and worse routing metrics
(OF) - see Fig.
11c. The idea behind this type is to disrupt
routing topology and introducing delays, by forcing nodes
into choosing further nodes as parents. However, The
effect of this type of attacks is low compared to the
previous type [
17].
• Worst Parent Attacks: This type is very hard to detect,
as the adversary will simply advertise its actual rank
but select the worst parent for itself - see Fig.
11d. The
results are similar to the previous type, and it can use the
decreased rank method to attract more nodes to select it
as a parent, while sending their packets to the worse path
[
17], [ 39], resulting in increased delays or even routing
loops [ 39].
Sahay et al. in [ 74] conducted an attack-graph-based assessment on the three types of rank attacks, and supported
their assessment with simulations. The results showed that the
increased rank attack introduced more energy consumption
and higher overhead trafﬁc, while the decreased rank attack
caused a large disruption in the network’s trafﬁc and the RPL’s
DODAG. Finally, the worst parent attack resulted in network
sub-optimization and increased the E2E delays, as expected.
Mitigation techniques: A lot of research was carried to
mitigate rank attacks, due to being one of the most effective
RPL-speciﬁc attacks. A summary is conducted in the following
and represented in Table
I:-
1) Acknowledgment-based Methods: Perrey et al. in [ 75],
[76] proposed Trust Anchor Interconnection Loop
(TRAIL) as a topology authentication mechanism. The
mechanism can be summarized as follows: Whenever
a DIO message is received, receiving node will send
a test message, that includes a random nonce and the
rank of DIO’s sender, through that sender toward the
root node. It will wait for a signed acknowledgment
from the root that includes the DODAG version, in
addition to the original nonce and the rank of the DIO’s
sender. All intermediate nodes should verify both the
test message and its acknowledgment for the ranks: The
DIO’s sender rank should be higher than its own, and
the previous hop’s rank should either lay between the
DIO sender’s rank and its own (for the test message),
or less than its own (for the acknowledgment). Failing
this validation will cause a discard of the message (or
the acknowledgment) and a start of a local repair. Once
received by the originating node, the acknowledgment’s
content will be veriﬁed and compared to the received
DIO message contents. If all ﬁelds match, the standard
RPL procedures will be carried on. If not, or no ac-

To minimize the overhead control and enable better scalability for TRAIL, the authors provided an aggregation
