2) Trust-based Methods: The mechanisms used for blackhole mitigation (see §
VI-A4) can also be used to mitigate rank attacks.
3) Statistical/Mathematical-based Methods:-
• IDSs of this type, such INTI (see § V -B), InDReS
(see §V -C) and game theory IDS (see§ V -D) are also
valid for rank attacks mitigation, using extensions.
• Dvir et al. in [ 77] proposed V ersion number and
Rank Authentication (V eRA) mechanism. A mechanism that uses one-way hash function to create two
related hash chains: one for version numbers and
another for associated rank numbers. The authors
evaluated their mechanism and showed its ability
to detect and mitigate all version number and decreased rank attacks. However, it was shown in
several reviews [
4], [ 17], [ 76], [ 78] that V eRA is
still vulnerable to rank forgery, by either creating a
forged rank hash chain or simply replying its parent
rank. In addition to that, the complexity of the V eRA
suggests a big increase in resources consumption
(energy and others), yet it has to be investigated.
• Iuchi et al. in [
78] introduced another mechanism
to mitigate rank attacks, named Secure Parent Node
Selection: each node will compute the Average Rank
from the DIO messages it received, then it uses
this average to create a Rank Threshold (based
on an equation and application-based parameter).
The node, then, will exclude any node that has
lower rank than the threshold from its possible
parent set, based on the fact that adversaries will
announce much lower rank value to attract most of
their neighbors. The system showed very interesting
results, mitigating 95% of the attack. However, it
was found to increase the convergence time slightly.
In addition, there is no energy nor resources consumption evaluation.
4) Speciﬁcation-based Methods: SVELTE (see §
V -A) and
RPL speciﬁcation-based IDS (see § V -E) can mitigate
rank attacks. In addition, the mechanism proposed by
Glissa [
46] (see § VI-A6) also can be used for rank
attacks mitigation.

B. V ersion Attacks
This attack is an exploit of the Global Repair feature of
RPL. As mentioned in § III-D, only the root node is supposed
to change the version number of the DODAG, but the current
RPL standard does not have any mechanisms to assure the
integrity of the communicated version number [
53].
If a malicious node sent a DIO message with a higher
version number, it will force the global repair procedure to
start, and create topology inconsistency and routing loops,
especially if the malicious node was far from the root node
[
79].
Aris et al. in [ 79] showed, experimentally, the different
effects of version attack on an RPL-based IoT network
realistic conﬁguration, using probabilistic attacker scheme
and considering mobility: packet delivery was down by more
than half the normal value, with the effect getting worse
when malicious nodes are near the leaves. Average delays
also increased six times, control overhead also was up by
7500% on average, and power consumption was increased
by 265%. It is clear that the main goal of version number
attacks is to exhaust nodes resources and slow down packet
delivery to a crawl.
Mitigation techniques:
1) Acknowledgment-based Methods: TRAIL (see previous
section §
VII-A1) proved to be very effective against
version number attacks.
2) Trust-based Methods: Same trust-based methods used
for blackhole attacks’ mitigation (see § VI-A4) are applicable to mitigate version number attacks.
3) Statistical/Mathematical-based Methods: InDReS (see
§V -C), Game theory IDS (see § V -D) and V eRA mechanism (see previous section § VII-A3) are all used for
version number attacks’ mitigation (IDSs requires extensions).
4) Speciﬁcation-based Methods: SVELTE (see §
V -A) and
RPL speciﬁcation-based IDS (see § V -E) can mitigate
version number attacks. In addition, distributed IDS
proposed by Mayzaud et al. (see §
V -F) was designed
to mitigate version attacks.
C. Local Repair Attacks
In this type of attacks, a malicious node will send local
repair messages to its neighbors periodically, without actually
having any problems. This will cause neighboring nodes to
recalculate their routes that go through the malicious node,
ending in constructing the same topology again. Clearly, the
main goal of this attack is to create much more control
overhead and exhaust nodes’ resources [
19]. Le et al. showed
in [ 18] that this attack could severely affect delivery ratio for
the network due to both increased control overhead and that
compromised nodes and its child will be involved in the local
repair process. However, it has less effect on the end-to-end
delay as the routes still have the optimal path as long as the
attack is not combined with other types of attacks [
39].
Mitigation techniques: To the best of our knowledge, there
is no dedicated mitigation method for the local repair attack
until this point. However, some proposed solutions for other
attacks and some IDSs can be extended/used to mitigate local
repair attacks:-
1) Acknowledgment-based Methods: Authors of TRAIL
(see §
VII-A1) suggested an extension to their mechanism to detect local repair attacks; however, it was not
described nor evaluated.
2) Trust-based Methods: Mechanisms proposed by Djedjig
et al. and Airehrour et al. (see §
VI-A4) had mentioned
a possibility to extend their mechanisms to detect other
types of attacks, including the local repair ones. However, no details nor evaluation were conducted.
3) Speciﬁcation-based Methods: Authors of SVELTE (see
§
V -A) mentioned an extension of their IDSs to detect
local repair attacks. In addition, RPL speciﬁcation-based
IDS (see §
V -E) can be used directly to mitigate these
attacks. However, it was shown that it starts with high
false negatives before the true detection rate increases
after some time [
48].
D. DIS Attacks
When a new node wants to join a DODAG, a DODAG
Information Solicitation (DIS) message is sent to inform
surrounding nodes and to get topology information from them
[
3]. An adversary could misuse this feature and periodically
send DIS messages to its neighbors. As mentioned in § III-A2,
the adversary can either:-
• Broadcast his DIS messages, forcing a local repair upon
neighboring nodes. Depending on the number of affected
neighboring nodes and the scale of the attack, some
anomaly-based or speciﬁcation-based IDSs can detect the
attack [
48].
• Unicast the DIS messages, causing unnecessary exchange
of unicasted DIO messages with the neighboring nodes
without resetting their trickle times [
18], [ 19].
In both cases, the adversary can send these DIS messages
using its own IP address or uses fake ones [ 18].
Le et al. [18] studied the effects of DIS attacks, unicasted
and broadcasted, in great details. They found that, while
DIS attacks do not affect delivery rates (the attack does
not change the topology), it does increase the end-to-end
delay signiﬁcantly. The broadcasted DIS attack speciﬁcally
can increase the delay by up to 200%, as it forces the
neighbors of the adversary to broadcast DIO messages to their
neighboring nodes. This process will create more affected
routes; hence, larger amounts of control overhead. On the
other hand, in the unicasted DIS attack, these neighbors will
unicast their DIO messages, thus produce less control overhead
than broadcasting method. This will reduce the possibility of
detecting the adversary by anomaly-based or speciﬁcationbased IDSs
Mitigation techniques: To the best of our knowledge, there
is no dedicated mitigation method for the attack till this point.
However, and as mentioned in §
III-A2, IETF proposed some
modiﬁcations to how nodes response to DIS message, in order
to reduce the effects of such attack or eliminate it all together.
These modiﬁcations are yet to be tested and investigated. In

E. Neighbor Attacks
In this attack, an adversary will forward any DIO message
it gets to its neighboring nodes, without modiﬁcation, creating the illusion that the original sender is in the range of
neighboring nodes [
18], [ 19] while it is not. A worst case
scenario is when the original sender has a good rank and
adversary’s neighbors choose it as their preferred parent, even
though it is out of their range. It is worth mentioning that while
neighbor attack occurs also in mobile Ad hoc networks [
80],
it is usually performed with all exchanged messages. Unlike
neighbor attacks in RPL, which are performed by forwarding
unmodiﬁed DIO messages only.
The effect of this attack by itself is not signiﬁcant, as it
results only in a slight increase of end-to-end delay [
18],
however combining this attack with other types of attacks
could make it more dangerous. For example, an adversary
could launch a DIS attack to get DIO messages with better
metrics, then selecting one of these messages to perform a
neighbor attack, increasing the effect of such an attack.
Another alteration to this attack is known as Route Information Replay attacks [
17]: an adversary can store control
messages from other nodes, then sends them later to its
neighbors, forcing them to update their routing tables with
outdated data and creating inconsistent topology [
17].
Mitigation techniques: It is very difﬁcult to detect this type
of attacks [ 18], [19], as its adversaries appear as transparent to
the network. To our best knowledge, there is a limited number
of literature on mitigating this type of attacks. Some IDSs
can be extended to detect these attacks, such as SVELTE
(see §
V -A) and RPL speciﬁcation-based IDS (see § V -E), as
mentioned by their authors [ 48], [ 51]. In addition, and in our
opinion, implementing a location-based mitigation method that
ties up location and transmission range information of the
nodes can theoretically help detecting the attack.
F . RPL Storing Mode Attacks
These attacks require that RPL run in storing mode – See
§
III-C – as they target stored downward routing tables [ 40].
In general, there are three types of these attacks:-
• Routing Table Overload Attacks: Since each node will
maintain its own routing table, an adversary can send
many bogus routes (via DAO messages) to the compromised node until it is saturated. This will prevent
the compromised node from accepting legitimate DAO
messages and build correct routes [
17]. To the best of
our knowledge, there is no detailed study of the effects
of this attack, only a few mentions in the literature [
20].
• DAO Inconsistency Attacks: DAO inconsistency and its
corresponding repair mechanism have been discussed in
§
III-D. However, an adversary can misuse this repair
mechanism and simply return any packet that it receives
with "F" ﬂag set. This will cause either a full isolation for
the sub-DODAG of the adversary, or creates sub-optimal

• Route Table F alsiﬁcation Attacks: a malicious node(s)

17]. This causes packet losses (in case
of forged routes) or longer end-to-end delays.
In addition to the effects mentioned above, these attacks
cause resource exhaustion for compromised nodes due to
increased control overhead and repetitive repair attempts [
17].
Mitigation techniques: To the best of our knowledge, there
is no proposed method to detect and mitigate RPL storingmode attacks, nor a detailed study of the effects of such attacks
on RPL networks. However, for DAO inconsistency attacks,
RFC6653 [
40] proposes to limit the rate of trickle timer resets
(due to discarding routes using the recovery method) to a
value, suggesting 20 resets per hour, as a method to limit
the effect of the attack. This method was challenged by Pu
in [
81], where the author showed that the adversaries still can
initiate the attack while not reaching this ﬁxed limit, and he
proposed a Dynamic Threshold Mechanism (DTM) to to set
the rate limit of the trickle timer reset. Simulations of DTM (as
carried out by the author) proved its efﬁciency, as it reduced
the control overhead, packet drops and energy consumption of
the network.
G. DODAG Inconsistency Attacks
In §
III-D, DAG inconsistency was discussed with its RPL’s
repair mechanism. It has been shown in [ 40], [ 82], [ 83] that
an attacker can misuse this mechanism to initiate an attack
against the network: An adversary could send inconsistent
packets (upward direction with "O" ﬂag set or downward
direction with "O" ﬂag clear) with the "R" ﬂag also set [
40],
or it could manipulate "O" and "R" ﬂags of any packet it
receives before forwarding it [
82]. The effects of DODAG inconsistency attacks are usually much higher control overhead,
energy/resources exhaustion, and longer end-to-end delays. In
case of using packet manipulation to initiate the attack, it
creates a blackhole-like situation that is very hard to detect
(legitimate next-hop node will always drop the packet and
start a local repair procedure), in addition to the previously
mentioned effects.
Mitigation techniques: There is no method to actively detect these attacks, according to our best knowledge. RFC6553
[
40] proposes to use the same limit on the rate of trickle

be signiﬁcantly reducing the control overhead, packet drops,
and energy consumption; with negligible effect on processing

H. Replay Attacks
The idea behind these attacks is to record legitimate control
messages (DIO, DIS, and DAO) and forward them later in the
network [
17], [ 20]. These attacks can be performed even with
RPL’s secure modes active, and the adversaries do not need
to know the used cryptography keys. Such attacks will result
in outdated or false routing entries, degraded routing service,
lower packet delivery rate, and in some cases detachment of
the victim’s sub-DODAG. It is worth noting that while replay
attacks occurs also in WSNs, it is usually performed with data
messages, not control messages. Unlike replay attacks in RPL,
which are mainly performed by replaying control messages.
A special type of replay attacks, the DIO Suppression
Attack, was recently discovered and detailed by Perazzo et
al. in [
84]. This attack uses the concept of replay attacks to
exploits the Trickle algorithm governing DODAG creation and
maintenance (see §
III-B). In its simplest form, the adversary
will replay a DIO message it heard from a legitimate node
(parent to the victim) several times with a ﬁxed frequency
that is enough to convince the victim that there are no
changes in the network and double its trickle timer; hence,
the attack suppresses the victim’s DIO message transmission.
This would lead to imperfect routing (degraded quality of
service) or, in the worst case scenario, a complete detachment
of the victim and its sub-DODAG from the network. The
authors described different techniques to launch the attack
in more complex situations; for example, when encryption is
implemented at the link layer or within RPL. Their evaluation
of the attack proved its signiﬁcant effect on degrading the
delivery rates of the network. However, it was proven also
that this attack can be mitigated by enabling RPL’s optional
replay protection mechanism (see §
III-E), even though this
mechanism would increase [ 43] network formation delay (3
to 4 times more), power consumption (around 18% increase),
and control overhead (around 10% increase).
In general, replay attacks have more damaging effects on
dynamic networks than on the static ones: adversaries can
record control messages from its neighbors at one end, then
move to another part of the network and replay these control
messages, forcing the new neighboring nodes to update their
routing tables with outdated and false information.
Mitigation techniques: RPL’s replay protection mechanism
is efﬁcient to mitigate the replay attacks in static networks.
However, and to the best of our knowledge, there are no
detection/mitigation proposals in the literature for the dynamic
networks. In addition, all current implementations of RPL (up
to the time of this writing) do not have the optional reply
protection mechanism.
Summary and Insights
Unlike WSN-inherited attacks, most of RPL-speciﬁc attacks
have larger impact on the network by themselves. The exceptions to this rule would be the DIS and neighbor attacks,
which effects only the the E2E delay. From a mitigation
methods’ point of view, speciﬁcation-based methods are the
most effective in regard of the mitigation of the attacks.
However, many of the current proposals of these methods does
not provide full details or complete implementations for the
mitigation of all RPL-speciﬁc attacks.
From our observation, there is an urgent need for more
research on detection and mitigation methods for DIS attack,
neighbor attack, RPL storing mode attacks, DODAG inconsistency attacks, and replay attacks in dynamic networks or
with mobile nodes. The literature covering such subject are
numbered and very limited. In addition, we believe that more
work should be conducted to incorporate some of the proposed
mitigation mechanisms into RPL’s standard, with optimization
for the different types of networks where RPL would be
deployed.
VIII. I SSUES , C HALLENGES , AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS
There is a great deal of research on mitigating routing
attacks in RPL-based networks, which shows clearly from
our survey. However, there are still many issues on how
researchers are approaching the mitigation solution. In the
following, we list the main issues and challenges we believe
are facing the research community, and suggest a few future
directions that should be used to approach the future work on
mitigating routing attacks in RPL.
1) Implementation of RPL’s Security Features: As we mentioned in §
III-E, even though RPL standard has numerous
pages on security features, it does mark them as "optional".
Hence, and to the best of our knowledge, no current implementation of RPL (that has been used in either real applications or
research work) has these security features, with the exception
of the partial implementation we found provided by Perazzo
et al. in [
43]. In essence, this means that the effects of such
security features on mitigating routing attacks has not been
assessed, which in our opinion may lead to eliminating the
need for added mechanisms to mitigate some attacks. We
believe that RPL’s security features should be one of the ﬁrst
tasks to investigate in any future research, with emphasis on
seeking ways of implementing the third mode of security:
Authenticated mode . Such implementation could be challenging for current platforms in terms of resource consumption;
however, with the rapid advancements in hardware, we think
that implementing the full list of RPL’s security features is
feasible.
There are also concerns that all recent literature does
not consider these security features in the research. Such

negligence could make many of the mitigation methods we
investigated have complex structures and procedures, which
results in more resource consumption at the nodes.
2) Effects of RPL’s Routing Attacks on Large Networks:
According to our best knowledge, there is little work on
studying the effects of routing attacks on large IoT networks
(> 250 nodes) [
22]. Large IoT networks with a large number
of nodes introduce many challenges on several levels, designwise and security-wise. On the security front, such networks
introduce larger delays to DODAG formation and maintenance
times, leaving devices exposed to several effective attacks such
as Sybil, replay attacks, or any location-based attacks [
85].
One suggestion is to consider the work of Abdou et al. in
[86] as one of the future directions that should be investigated
for adaptation in IoT networks, as it focuses on verifying the
locations of the nodes in large networks.
In addition, the large number of RPL control messages
used would render these networks jammed for long periods
of times (with larger control trafﬁc if the optional replay
protection mechanism is implemented [
43]), which could
be used by some adversaries as a DoS attack. The current
approaches [
87]–[90], among many others, are trying to solve
this challenge. However, those papers have not shown much
detail on how this will affect the security of these networks.
3) The Large Number of Optional Features in RPL: RPL
standard has so many optional features to provide ﬂexibility for
many different applications. However, this would lead to many
incompatible implementations as each will have a different
set of the optional features [
21]. From the security point of
view, having several implementations to communicate with
each other could lead to unintentional or even undiscovered
attacks. For example, when a network works in non-storing
MOP and a new sub-DODAG is joining the network but with
an implementation that uses storing MOP , or when nodes use
different OFs. We would argue the need for streamlining the
standard and eliminate the never-used (or outdated features).
This would lead to much more consistent implementations.
4) Adaptation of RPL to Different Networks Environments
(such as Machine-to-Machine, Smart Grids, etc.): As the IoT
world expands, new network environments are introduced;
hence, new requirements for routing and security arise. For
example, there have been a lot of work on Advanced Metering
Infrastructure (AMI) and M2M networks recently. With RPL
becoming the standard routing protocol for IoT networks,
many researchers are looking into adapting RPL to the unique
requirements of these networks [
91]–[94]. Aijaz et al. in [ 95],
[96] proposed Cognitive and Opportunistic RPL (CORPL) to
adapt RPL for the Cognitive Radio (CR) based smart grid
networks. CORPL introduces opportunistic forwarder set (parents) election and coordination scheme for the best forwarder
selection. That has been done by using a specially crafted
OF and some modiﬁcations to RPL and MAC layer protocol.
The authors evaluated the proposed protocol using MA TLAB
simulations, and it showed that CORPL signiﬁcantly enhanced
the packet delivery rate when compared to regular RPL,
specially in poor link conditions. However, no evaluation for
the performance of the protocol under routing attacks was
performed.
Another approach to routing in AMI and smart grid networks, this time proposed by IETF, is the Ad-hoc On-demand
Distance V ector (AODV)-RPL [
97]. Currently an Internetdraft, the proposed protocol combine the reactive nature of
routing on demand in AODV with the normal RPL operation.
The intention is to provide AMI networks with asymmetrical
bidirectional paths between the originating and target nodes
when the normal RPL path is not available or not reliable
enough. Since it is in early stages of development, no evaluation was conducted on the protocol’s performance nor it
resilience against security threats.
From this observation, we believe there is an urgent need
to conduct more research on the security requirements in
these new environments, and to evaluate the effects of routing
attacks on them. Also, it is necessary to investigate how
RPL (with all the proposed modiﬁcations to adapt the new
environments) will react to the attacks that are normally
available only in these environment.
5) Scalability of IDSs for RPL: Most of the IDSs discussed
in this survey have a margin for extensibility, to add detection
capability of attacks that were not in the original design
of the IDS. However, the details of such extensions and
their evaluation were not provided in most of the reviewed
literature. In addition, none of RPL’s own security features
were considered at the design of all IDSs, as to the best of
our knowledge. In our investigation, we came to a belief that
an extensible IDS which uses the authenticated security mode
of RPL to mitigate routing attacks would be efﬁcient; however,
no such IDS is currently proposed. Hence, it should be on the
top of the list of research directions.
6) Advancements in Platform’s Hardware: As found during
our investigation and conﬁrmed by [
21], most of the evaluation
presented in the literature is carried out with simulation
on ContikiOS or TinyOS, and they used either the TelosB
[
98] or Zolertia Z1 [ 99] platforms. Both platforms are now
considered outdated; TelosB has pre-2010 components and
Zolertia Z1 has been retired by its manufacturer and replaced
with updated motes. This would represent a challenge in that
the necessary mitigation methods and security features cannot
be implemented in a fully functional state (due to higher
resources consumption). We believe that more recent hardware
platform, such as the ones based on Cortex-M3 [
100], should
be used for both real-world or simulated evaluation.
In our opinion, the current pace of advancements in electronic has made it much easier to have new platforms with
multi-core processors that can handle complex operations and
are energy efﬁcient, larger capacity batteries that are small in
size, and higher storage sizes that can store several gigabytes
of valuable data, all within the same chipset. This would make
future implementation of security features or measurements
much more feasible and would lead to more research in this
area.
IX. C ONCLUSION
We have thoroughly reviewed the RPL’s standard, including
the recent IETF proposal of modiﬁcation to the standard.
The recently published attacks on RPL were classiﬁed and

investigated thoroughly, showing up-to-date results from recent
literature. In addition, we studied the mitigation methods of
these attacks, and introduced a technique-based classiﬁcation scheme for the mitigation methods. Besides, IDSs were
discussed and comprehensively investigated, with the most
inﬂuential ones highlighted. In our survey, RPL is shown to be
prone to various vulnerabilities, and it is clear that its security
still needs more research. A hybrid-detection IDS with hybrid
placement, such as the SVELTE and RPL’s Speciﬁcation-based
IDSs, is the best solution to mitigate several RPL attacks at the
same time, as shown in our survey. However, more research
should be conducted in order to further reduce and optimize
resource usage of the IDSs to accommodate the constrained
resources of IoT devices. In addition, RPL’s security features
should be evaluated for attacks’ mitigation.
We believe that this survey would provide a solid background for future researchers seeking to investigate and develop better mitigation methods for RPL-based IoT networks.

Ahmed Raoof is a graduate from University of
Benghazi, Libya; where he received both of his
B.Sc. degree (Electrical and Electronic Engineering,
2003) and his M.Sc. degree (Telecomm. Engineering, 2009). After which, he worked as an Assistant
Lecturer at the Faculty of Information Technology,
University of Benghazi from 2009 to 2013, where
he taught computer networks and communications
courses and supervised several ﬁnal year projects.
Currently, he is pursuing the Ph.D. degree with the
System and Computer Engineering department at
Carleton University. His research interests include network protocols, security
of computer networks and Internet of Things (IoT), and edge computing.
Ashraf Matrawy Ashraf Matrawy is a Professor
at the School of Information Technology, Carleton
University. He is a senior member of the IEEE
and serves on the editorial board of the IEEE
Communications Surveys and Tutorials journal. He
has served as a technical program committee member of IEEE CNS, IEEE ICC, IEEE Globecom,
IEEE LCN, and IEEE/ACM CCGRID. He is also
a Network co-Investigator of Smart Cybersecurity
Network (SERENE-RISC). His research interests
include reliable and secure computer networking,
software deﬁned networking and cloud computing.
Chung-Horng Lung Chung-Horng Lung received
the B.S. degree in Computer Science and Engineering from Chung-Y uan Christian University, Taiwan
and the M.S. and Ph.D. degrees in Computer Science and Engineering from Arizona State University. He was with Nortel Networks from 1995 to
2001. In September 2001, he joined the Department
of Systems and Computer Engineering, Carleton
University, Ottawa, Canada, where he is now a
Professor. His research interests include: Communication Networks, Software Engineering, and Cloud
Computing.
